episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Working Lands & Industry
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Business Insider] A consulting firm is now using AI to give free advice that once cost millions and [CBC Arts] Hasbro releases AI versions of its iconic characters, including Transformers, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Who captures the economic value generated by resource extraction and infrastructure development in the Cariboo-Chilcotin region, and how can communities ensure they retain benefits rather than bearing costs? Topics include: Carbon markets as mechanisms for pricing ecosystem services and their volatility risks, Ownership structure differences between Indigenous joint ventures and external contractor models, Data centre infrastructure costs to watersheds and power systems versus concentrated financial benefits, Asset ownership versus management contracts as pathways to permanent value capture, Technical and institutional capacity constraints preventing communities from negotiating favorable terms.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd.

discussion.central_question: Who captures the economic value generated by resource extraction and infrastructure development in the Cariboo-Chilcotin region, and how can communities ensure they retain benefits rather than bearing costs?
discussion.riley_position: Provincial forest resiliency funding and emerging carbon markets represent meaningful interventions that redistribute value back to regions and Indigenous Nations, even if imperfect; the underlying logic of pricing ecosystem services is sound and gaining traction.
riley_key_evidence: Tŝilhqot'in National Government and Great Bear Rainforest carbon agreements generated real revenue for coastal Nations through conservation value capture
BC forest resiliency funding of sixteen Cariboo-Chilcotin projects represents provincial money flowing back into the region as redistribution
Carbon markets are creating mechanisms to price what was previously unpriced, even if subject to volatility
discussion.casey_position: Project-level grants and carbon market mechanisms address symptoms but not the structural problem—value must be prevented from escaping in the first place through permanent community ownership of assets rather than temporary management contracts.
casey_key_evidence: Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation's Nation-owned joint venture keeps biomass contract revenue local, contrasting with Vancouver-based contractors extracting value
BC hydro's carbon accounting excludes embedded ecological costs (fish passage, flood cycles, salmon run impacts) paid by Interior Nations but not disclosed on data centre carbon footprints
Tŝilhqot'in Supreme Court decision on territorial title established legal basis for benefiting from resources, but financial instruments and community technical capacity remain gaps
discussion.resolution: They reached agreement on the distinction between project-level redistribution and structural asset ownership without fully resolving the debate; both acknowledged that permanence through asset ownership (rather than grant cycles) is the missing piece, and agreed this framework should shape how communities approach future funding announcements and policy negotiations.
topics_covered: Carbon markets as mechanisms for pricing ecosystem services and their volatility risks
Ownership structure differences between Indigenous joint ventures and external contractor models
Data centre infrastructure costs to watersheds and power systems versus concentrated financial benefits
Asset ownership versus management contracts as pathways to permanent value capture
Technical and institutional capacity constraints preventing communities from negotiating favorable terms
